hints.to seem: 'To seem' is 'videri' (passive of 'videre') — active 'video' means 'I see'

hints.perfect indicative: Perfect would be 'vidi' — 'Video' with the -eo ending is present indicative
hints.future indicative: Future would be 'videbo' — 'Video' is present indicative
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'videam' — 'Video' is present indicative

hints.vinco, vincere, vici, victum: These are the parts of 'vincere' (to conquer)
hints.vivo, vivere, vixi, victum: These are the parts of 'vivere' (to live)
hints.vito, vitare, vitavi, vitatum: These are the parts of 'vitare' (to avoid)

steps.prompt: This sentence uses indirect discourse. What is the construction?

hints.ut + subjunctive: 'Ut' + subjunctive is for purpose or result — 'video' takes acc. + inf. for indirect statement
hints.quod + indicative: 'Quod' + indicative can introduce facts, but 'video' here governs acc. + infinitive
hints.relative clause of characteristic: There is no relative pronoun — this is acc. + inf. indirect discourse

hints.nominative: Nominative masculine would be 'duo' — 'duos' is accusative masculine plural

hints.to eat: 'To eat' is 'esse/edere' (from 'edo') — this 'esse' is from 'sum' (to be)
hints.to go out: 'To go out' is 'exire' — 'esse' means to be

steps.prompt: What tense is the infinitive 'esse' in this indirect discourse?
steps.reveal: esse = to be / that there are (present inf.)

hints.perfect infinitive: Perfect infinitive would be 'fuisse' — 'esse' is the present infinitive
hints.future infinitive: Future infinitive would be 'futurum esse' or 'fore' — 'esse' is present
hints.imperfect infinitive: Latin has no 'imperfect infinitive' — the three infinitive tenses are present, perfect, and future

hints.edo, esse, edi, esum: These are the parts of 'edere' (to eat) — confusingly, its infinitive is also 'esse'
hints.eo, ire, ii, itum: These are the parts of 'ire' (to go)
hints.fero, ferre, tuli, latum: These are the parts of 'ferre' (to carry)

hints.nominative: Nominative plural would be 'sententiae' — 'sententias' with -as is accusative plural
hints.genitive: Genitive singular would be 'sententiae' — 'sententias' is accusative plural
hints.ablative: Ablative plural would be 'sententiis' — 'sententias' is accusative plural

hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'sententiae' ends in -ae
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'sententiae' ends in -ae
hints.5th declension: 5th declension has genitive in -ei — 'sententiae' ends in -ae

steps.prompt: In the Fourth Catilinarian, what 'two proposals' is Cicero referring to?

hints.exile vs. acquittal: Exile was not proposed — the debate was between execution and perpetual imprisonment
hints.war vs. negotiation: The military question was separate — this debate concerned the arrested conspirators' fate
hints.trial vs. summary execution: Both proposals bypassed a regular trial — the question was death vs. imprisonment

translation: I see that there are two proposals
